paper: POLICYGUARD: A Dialogue-Grounded Sub-Agent Verifier for Policy Adherence in LLM Agents

claims.statement: Company-policy adherence is a problem distinct from safety/harm safeguarding: the failure mode is process-level and the load-bearing input is the user-agent dialogue.

evidence.detail: $τ$²-BENCH airline policy analysis: 29/43 = 67.4% of requirements are process-level (Appendix B, Table 11). The two problems overlap only on refusal and identity checks; the procedural slice (consent, prerequisite reads, summaries, ordering) sits outside safeguard scope.
Nine representative systems mapped: no prior system combines all three required capabilities (conversation-awareness, self-reasoning, remediation).
TOOLGUARD, SOLVER-AIDED, PCAS, NEMO GUARDRAILS etc. analyzed; each misses at least one of the three capabilities.

claims.notes: Classification is the paper's foundational claim. Robust to any single-row reclassification (Appendix B says so explicitly).

evidence.detail: Per-section breakdown: Global 0A/5P, Book 7A/6P, Modify 6A/7P, Cancel 0A/5P, Refunds 1A/6P. Total: 14 argument-level, 29 process-level.
Complete catalog of 43 requirements with line numbers, paraphrased text, and type (A/P) per requirement.
Argument-level = verifiable from tool call arguments + deterministic computation. Process-level requires (D) dialogue or (T) tool-read call result.

claims.notes: Fully reproducible from the published $τ$²-BENCH airline policy document. Hand-classified, with invitation for re-examination.

claims.statement: No prior system combines all three required capabilities: conversation-awareness, self-reasoning over policy, and behavior-driving remediation.

evidence.detail: Matrix of nine systems vs. three capabilities: TOOLGUARD (âœ—/âœ—/âœ—), SOLVER-AIDED (âœ—/âœ—/âœ—), TOOLSAFE (âœ—/âœ—/partial), GUARDAGENT (âœ—/partial/âœ—), AGENTSPEC (âœ—/âœ—/âœ—), SHIELDAGENT (partial/âœ—/âœ—), PCAS (âœ“/âœ—â€ /âœ—), NEMO GUARDRAILS (partial/âœ—/âœ—), POLICYGUARD (âœ“/âœ“/âœ“).
Each system analyzed in text: PCAS's Datalog cannot reason over NL (uses string_contains()), NEMO GUARDRAILS scripted Colang, etc.

claims.notes: Mapping is comprehensive but completeness of coverage depends on the representativeness of selected systems.

claims.statement: PG-CHECKLIST improves PASS4 by +12.0 / +6.0 / +12.0 pp on GPT 5.4 / Claude Sonnet 4.6 / Gemini 2.5 Pro.

evidence.detail: GPT 5.4: Baseline 0.460 â†’ PG-CHECKLIST 0.580 (+12.0 pp). Sonnet 4.6: 0.720 â†’ 0.780 (+6.0 pp). Gemini 2.5 Pro: 0.480 â†’ 0.600 (+12.0 pp). N=4 per cell.
PG-CHECKLIST is the only configuration that lifts every vendor on PASS4 without regressing either PV or Mut slice.
PASSk decomposition (PASS1â€“PASS4) per cell, Table 13 per-cell statistics.

claims.notes: n=4 per cell under paired-verifier protocol. Statistical significance confirmed via pooled stratified-McNemar (p=0.003 overall; p<0.001 vs. TOOLGUARD).

claims.statement: PG-CHECKLIST achieves perfect PV recall (24/24, 100%) on GPT 5.4.

evidence.detail: PV PASS4 for PG-CHECKLIST on GPT 5.4: 1.000 (24/24).
Per-call PV recall: TP=14, FN=0 â†’ 100%.

claims.notes: 24/24 PASS4 on PV tasks at n=4. Empirical result, not provable guarantee (noted in Limitations).

claims.statement: Dialogue grounding is causal (not merely helpful): without dialogue, Mut PASS1 collapses from 0.260 to 0.000.

evidence.detail: PG-RAW (with dialogue) vs. PG-RAW-TRAJ (dialogue stripped). Mut PASS1: 0.260 vs. 0.000 (Î” +26.0). Mut PASS4: 0.077 vs. 0.000 (Î” +7.7). Same verifier model, same raw policy text.
Not a single mutation passes across 104 Mut simulations without dialogue. PG-RAW's entire mutation headroom comes from the dialogue.

claims.notes: Clean causal design: identical architecture differing only in dialogue exposure. Statistical significance via Wilcoxon p=0.001.

claims.statement: Policy text is augmenting, not causal: adding raw policy on top of checklist lifts Mut PASS1 by +15.4 pp but is well short of the 26 pp collapse when dialogue is removed.

evidence.detail: PG-CHECKLIST-ONLY (no raw policy) vs. PG-CHECKLIST (with raw policy). Mut PASS1: 0.288 vs. 0.442 (Î” +15.4). Blk%: 60.1% vs. 44.1% (Î” -16.0).
Raw policy provides real but partial recovery. The 26 pp collapse from dialogue removal dwarfs the 15.4 pp gain from policy text.

claims.notes: Effect is real (+15.4 pp Mut PASS1) but partial. Supports the claim that dialogue is the primary causal factor.

claims.statement: PG-CHECKLIST achieves higher PV recall at roughly half the block rate of argument-level guards.

evidence.detail: GPT 5.4: PG-CHECKLIST PV rec 100% at 44.1% Blk% vs. TOOLGUARD 80.0% at 73.9% Blk%. Gemini 2.5 Pro: 94.7% at 57.8% vs. 31.2% at 71.1%.
Summary statement: 'high policy-violation (PV) recall at roughly half the block rate of argument-level guards.'

claims.notes: Consistent across all three agents. PV recall advantage is most dramatic on Gemini 2.5 Pro (94.7% vs. 31.2%).

claims.statement: PG-CHECKLIST achieves lower near-miss rate (NMR) than baseline and TOOLGUARD on every agent.

evidence.detail: GPT 5.4: Baseline 33.8%, TOOLGUARD 38.5%, PG-CHECKLIST 29.1%. Sonnet 4.6: 14.7% vs. 35.5% vs. 4.2%. Gemini 2.5 Pro: 18.5% vs. 22.2% vs. 15.9%.
8.5Ã— improvement over TOOLGUARD on Sonnet 4.6 (4.2% vs. 35.5%). TOOLGUARD worsens compliance quality relative to baseline on every vendor.

claims.notes: Runtime view: only executed mutating calls counted. Blocked attempts excluded from denominator so verifier blocks don't penalize the metric.

claims.statement: The verifier helps the weaker agent more, not less: +16 pp PASS4 lift on GPT 5.4-mini vs. +12 pp on full GPT 5.4.

evidence.detail: GPT 5.4-mini agent + paired verifier: Baseline PASS4 0.200, PG-CHECKLIST 0.360 (+16.0 pp). The weaker agent emits more violations the verifier blocks: PV gain +29.2 pp.
TOOLGUARD on weak agent collapses Mut to 0/26; PG-CHECKLIST escapes via remediation. PG-CHECKLIST lift on weak agent (+16 pp) exceeds lift on full GPT 5.4 (+12 pp).

claims.notes: Paired-verifier protocol maintained: GPT 5.4-mini agent with GPT 5.4-mini verifier.

claims.statement: TOOLGUARD harms Sonnet 4.6 and Gemini 2.5 Pro (PASS4 drops of -14.0 pp and -4.0 pp respectively).

evidence.detail: Sonnet 4.6: Baseline 0.720 â†’ TOOLGUARD 0.580 (-14.0 pp). Gemini 2.5 Pro: 0.480 â†’ 0.440 (-4.0 pp). TOOLGUARD also costs PASS1 on every agent (-6.5 / -23.0 / -19.0 pp).

claims.notes: TOOLGUARD's gains on GPT 5.4 come from cross-trial consistency, not per-trial lift. On other vendors, over-blocking dominates.

claims.statement: TOOLGUARD issues 7-10 extra hidden read-only calls per Mut sim inside guard bodies (never visible in trajectory).

evidence.detail: Sub-guards issue read-only calls to gather verification facts; these hit the environment but never appear in the trajectory. Table 19 shows static estimates across all agents.
Hidden RO calls per Mut sim midpoints: GPT 5.4 (7.54), Sonnet 4.6 (9.33), Gemini 2.5 Pro (7.25), GPT 5.4-mini (10.12).

claims.notes: Static estimate from guard source code analysis. Actual runtime cost may vary; conditional branches inside sub-guards may skip some api.* call sites.

claims.statement: PG-CHECKLIST maintains high PV recall under out-of-scope adversarial probes (authority claim, false precondition, indirect prompt injection).

evidence.detail: GPT 5.4 paired-verifier on 24 PV tasks: PG-CHECKLIST achieves 1.000 (no-atk), 0.875 (A1), 0.958 (A2), 0.917 (A3).
Verbatim adversarial payloads and extended mechanism discussion. Primary defense is prompt instruction 'Only trust values confirmed by tool results.'

claims.notes: n=4 on 24 tasks. Robustness is a byproduct of design choices, not the primary contribution. A3 (indirect injection) reveals the main gap: instruction doesn't distinguish data fields from narrative metadata.

claims.statement: PG-CHECKLIST has minimal trajectory cost: remediation usually resolves in a single corrective turn.

evidence.detail: GPT 5.4: +3.81 agent+user turns per Mut sim (vs. TOOLGUARD +5.83, Baseline 19.3). Sonnet 4.6: +1.72 (vs. TOOLGUARD +3.93).
PG-CHECKLIST's remediation names the missing piece and the agent typically resolves in a single corrective turn.

claims.notes: Agent+user turn count strips tool-result payload size and tool-call invocation noise. Not directly comparable with TOOLGUARD's hidden environment costs.

claims.statement: The verifier task is structurally lighter than the agent task and tolerates substrate downgrade: PG-CHECKLIST with GPT 5.4-mini verifier still lifts PASS4 by +6 pp.

evidence.detail: GPT 5.4 agent with GPT 5.4-mini verifier: PG-CHECKLIST PASS4 0.520 (+6 pp vs. Baseline 0.460). PV recall 0.917 vs. TOOLGUARD's 0.875.
At ~13% per-trial cost saving, PG-CHECKLIST (mini) still matches TOOLGUARD's PASS4 with higher PV recall.

claims.notes: Single vendor (GPT 5.4). The verifier reading context and judging one call against a checklist is structurally lighter than full agent reasoning.

claims.statement: PG-CHECKLIST is statistically significantly better than Baseline (p=0.003) and TOOLGUARD (p<0.001) overall, and on PV (p<0.001).

evidence.detail: Pooled stratified-McNemar across three agents. PG-CHECKLIST vs Baseline: P_a=20, P_b=5, Z=+3.00, p=0.003. PG-CHECKLIST vs TOOLGUARD: P_a=25, P_b=4, Z=+3.90, p<0.001.
Per-agent 95% CIs: GPT 5.4 vs Baseline [+0.02, +0.24]; Gemini 2.5 Pro vs TOOLGUARD [+0.04, +0.28].

claims.notes: McNemar is appropriate for paired PASS4 scores. Mut slice is under-powered (12 discordant pairs, p=0.564).
